Do you take milk in your coffee?
This container will take (= has room for) six litres.
Which newspaper do you take (= regularly buy)?
He continually abuses her, and she just sits there and takes it.
If you think I'm going to take that lying down (= accept it without complaining), you're very much mistaken.
I take the/your point (= accept the argument), but I still don't think you should have gone.

definitions.definition: used when you want to mention something as a particular example of what you are talking about
examples: I've been very busy recently. Take last week, I had meetings every night after work, except Friday.

definitions.definition: If you take someone or something to be something, or if you take someone or something for something, you accept or believe that they are that thing
examples: These creatures are generally taken to be descended from primitive fishes.
I could have taken him for (= believed that he was) your brother.
I'm not going to forge his signature for you! What do you take me for? (= You should not believe I could do a thing like that.)

definitions.definition: to not be able to deal with an unpleasant situation
examples: I can't take it any more. I'm leaving.

definitions.definition: accept or refuse the offer completely
examples: That's my final offer - you can take it or leave it.

definitions.definition: to move in order to hold something in the hand(s)
examples: Can you take this bag while I open the door?
He took my arm and led me outside.
Take an egg and break it into the bowl.
He took hold of the plant's root and pulled.

definitions.definition: to go somewhere with someone, often paying for that person or being responsible for them
examples: We're taking the kids to the zoo on Saturday.
I took my elderly parents to look at some new houses.
Will you take me swimming tomorrow?

definitions.definition: to show someone how to get to somewhere by going there with them
examples: Let me take you to your room.

definitions.definition: to go to a social event with someone
examples: Who's taking you to the dance?

definitions.definition: to travel somewhere by using a particular form of transport or a particular vehicle, route, etc.
examples: I always take the train - it's less hassle than a car.
She took the 10.30 flight to Edinburgh.
If you take the road on the left, you'll come to the post office.

definitions.definition: to need
examples: Parachuting takes a lot of nerve.
Transitive verbs take a direct object.
His story took some believing (= was difficult to believe).

definitions.definition: to wear a particular size of clothes or shoes
examples: What size shoes do you take?

definitions.definition: If something takes a particular time, that period is needed in order to complete it
examples: The cooking process only takes ten minutes.
How long does this paint take to dry?
It took us all day to drive home.

definitions.definition: to act or happen over a short period of time
examples: I'm just going to the shops - I won't take long.

definitions.definition: to need a long time
examples: Broken bones always take time to heal.

definitions.definition: to measure something
examples: Nurses took my temperature and blood pressure.

definitions.definition: to swallow or use a medicine or drug, especially in a regular way
examples: Take this medicine three times a day.
Do you think she takes drugs?

definitions.definition: to do or perform
examples: Shelley is taking (= studying) economics at university.
The Archbishop took our service of thanksgiving.
Mr Marshall takes us for (= teaches us) physics.

definitions.definition: used with many nouns to make a verb phrase that is equal in meaning to the related verb
examples: I think we'll take a break (= we'll stop for a break) there.
If you're tired you should take a rest (= you should rest).
I always like to take a walk (= to walk) after lunch.

definitions.definition: to think about something or someone in a particular way
examples: We're taking the bomb threats very seriously.

definitions.definition: to understand something in a particular way
examples: I was just teasing him, but he took it the wrong way.
I'm not sure whether to take that as a compliment or an insult.
She gave a nod, which we took to mean that she agreed.

definitions.definition: to have or come to have a particular feeling or opinion
examples: He doesn't take any interest in his children.
Don't take any notice of the cameras.
She takes offence too easily.
They took pity on the stray cat and fed it.
I take the view that fuel should be heavily taxed to reduce road use.

definitions.definition: to surprise someone
examples: The sudden noise took her by surprise.

definitions.definition: to receive money from sales or as payment for entrance to an event
examples: The show took an astonishing $100,000 in its first week.

definitions.definition: to get possession of something or someone
examples: Rebels ambushed the train and took several prisoners.
The Liberals need just 200 more votes to take the seat from Labour.
With these wins, the Democrats have now taken the House back from the Republicans.
Centre-left parties look set to take power.
Adam, I'd like you to take control of the aircraft now.

definitions.definition: to write
examples: I hope you're all taking notes.

definitions.definition: to work or perform as expected
examples: These new plants haven't taken - they don't like this dry soil.

definitions.definition: money that is received from sales or as payment for entrance to an event
examples: The box office take was huge for the new show.

definitions.definition: the filming of a scene (= small part of a film)
examples: This scene needed ten takes before we felt it was right.

definitions.definition: an opinion about something
examples: We'd love to hear your take on this issue.

definitions.definition: a version of something that shows someone's attitude towards it
examples: The restaurant offers a new take on traditional French cooking.

definitions.definition: a main message or piece of information that you learn from something you hear or read
examples: The takeaway from the conference was how competitive the tourism industry has become.
At the end of the class, the teacher gives us the takeaway points.

definitions.definition: a meal cooked and bought at a shop or restaurant but taken somewhere else, often home, to be eaten, or the shop or restaurant itself
examples: a Chinese takeaway

examples: He said they had put a major dent in drug sales in the community with the takedown of the gang.

definitions.definition: an occasion when someone is defeated, especially in a sport
examples: the 6-4, 7-5, 6-3 takedown of the Swedish newcomer by the more experienced Swiss player

definitions.definition: an occasion when a website or document is officially removed from the internet
examples: Last year a takedown notice targeting a single site parodying the U.S. Chamber of Commerce resulted in a takedown of the websites of over 300 activist organizations.

definitions.definition: the moment when an aircraft leaves the ground and begins to fly

definitions.definition: used before numbers that refer to periods of ten years

definitions.definition: used before comparative adjectives or adverbs when you want to show that someone or something has become more or less of a particular state

definitions.definition: used for emphasis when you are expressing a strong opinion about someone or something

examples: Could you tie this piece of string for me?

examples: Is the allergy tied to dairy products, for example?

definitions.definition: (held or kept together) firmly or closely
examples: I can't untie the knot - it's too tight.
This lid is on very tight.
The people stood talking in tight groups.
Hold on tight when we go around the corner.
Check that windows and doors are shut tight (= completely closed) before you leave.
The plastic cover was stretched tight (= stretched as much as it could be) across the tank.
It is a tight squeeze on some trains, the General Manager acknowledged (= passengers are packed closely together).

definitions.definition: Clothes or shoes that are tight fit the body too closely and are uncomfortable
examples: That jacket's too tight - you need a bigger size.

definitions.definition: A tight feeling is an uncomfortable feeling of pressure in part of your body, caused by illness, fear, etc.
examples: Some people experienced tight chests after exercising in areas of high pollution.
If you have any symptoms such as itchy mouth or tight throat after eating nuts, stop.

definitions.definition: Tight muscles feel stiff and difficult to move
examples: If you sit for long periods, it can make your hamstring muscles very tight on the back of your thighs.
Calf stretches can alleviate tight calves when walking.

definitions.definition: looking or sounding as if you are angry, frightened, or nervous, and not relaxed
examples: In a small, tight voice, she said, "Help me, please."

definitions.definition: Tight controls or rules severely limit what can happen
examples: Rules on what can be taken on board an aircraft have become tighter.
tight security

definitions.definition: curving sharply, not gradually or loosely
examples: He lost control of his car on a tight bend.
The whales were swimming in a tight circle.
She was instantly recognizable with her short, tight curls.

definitions.definition: If a race, competition, election, etc. is tight, the people, teams, or organizations involved are of a similar standard and equally likely to be successful.
examples: The new governor was elected after an unexpectedly tight race.
The race was extremely tight with the lead changing twice in the last 250m.

definitions.definition: If time or money is tight, there is only just enough of it
examples: I'm sorry I can't stay for a chat, time's really tight.
They're raising three kids on one small salary so money is very tight.

definitions.definition: unwilling to spend money
examples: He was too tight to pay for a taxi so we had to walk.

definitions.definition: having drunk too much alcohol
examples: Jim, you're tight!

definitions.definition: If two people are tight, they know each other very well and like each other a lot
examples: The two of them were pretty tight at school.

definitions.definition: to become tighter or to make something become tighter, firmer, or less easy to move
examples: Tighten the straps so they don't rub.
As he struggled, the ropes tightened even more.

to become tighter or to make something become tighter, firmer, or less easy to move

definitions.definition: the activity of limiting the amount of money that people and companies are able to borrow or spend, for example by increasing interest rates
examples: There is pressure on the Bank of England to increase the pace of monetary tightening.
A tightening of US monetary policy has always produced a major slowdown in the economy.

definitions.definition: the action of making a rule, system, or law stronger and more difficult to ignore
examples: There has been a tightening of controls over domestic media and the internet.
The regulator is expected to announce a tightening up of the rules.

definitions.definition: a situation in a market where there is less of something available than is wanted or needed
examples: The supply and demand pendulum for some metals has swung so far that further tightening of the market seems inevitable.

examples: Tighten the straps so they don't rub.

definitions.definition: (held or kept together) firmly or closely
examples: I can't untie the knot - it's too tight.
This lid is on very tight.
The people stood talking in tight groups.
Hold on tight when we go around the corner.
Check that windows and doors are shut tight (= completely closed) before you leave.
The plastic cover was stretched tight (= stretched as much as it could be) across the tank.
It is a tight squeeze on some trains, the General Manager acknowledged (= passengers are packed closely together).

definitions.definition: Clothes or shoes that are tight fit the body too closely and are uncomfortable
examples: That jacket's too tight - you need a bigger size.

definitions.definition: A tight feeling is an uncomfortable feeling of pressure in part of your body, caused by illness, fear, etc.
examples: Some people experienced tight chests after exercising in areas of high pollution.
If you have any symptoms such as itchy mouth or tight throat after eating nuts, stop.

definitions.definition: Tight muscles feel stiff and difficult to move
examples: If you sit for long periods, it can make your hamstring muscles very tight on the back of your thighs.
Calf stretches can alleviate tight calves when walking.

definitions.definition: looking or sounding as if you are angry, frightened, or nervous, and not relaxed
examples: In a small, tight voice, she said, "Help me, please."

definitions.definition: Tight controls or rules severely limit what can happen
examples: Rules on what can be taken on board an aircraft have become tighter.
tight security

definitions.definition: curving sharply, not gradually or loosely
examples: He lost control of his car on a tight bend.
The whales were swimming in a tight circle.
She was instantly recognizable with her short, tight curls.

definitions.definition: If a race, competition, election, etc. is tight, the people, teams, or organizations involved are of a similar standard and equally likely to be successful.
examples: The new governor was elected after an unexpectedly tight race.
The race was extremely tight with the lead changing twice in the last 250m.

definitions.definition: If time or money is tight, there is only just enough of it
examples: I'm sorry I can't stay for a chat, time's really tight.

definitions.definition: unwilling to spend money
examples: He was too tight to pay for a taxi so we had to walk.

definitions.definition: having drunk too much alcohol

definitions.definition: the part of existence that is measured in minutes, days, years, etc., or this process considered as a whole
examples: He wants to spend more time with his family.
Time passes so quickly when you're having fun.
She grew more and more fascinated by the subject as time went on/by.
The curtains have faded over/with time (= as years have gone past).
You'll forget her in time (= in the future).
Over the course of time (= as years have gone past), holes have formed in the rock.
When Paula was ill, I took her some magazines to help her pass the time.
If you'd taken more time with/over (= spent more time doing) this essay, you could have done it much better.
It takes a long time (= many hours are needed) to get from London to Sydney.
We'd save time on our journey (= it would be quicker) if we went by train.
I only worked there for a short period of time.
The kitchen clock is gaining/losing time (= is going fast/slow).
My watch has never kept very good time (= been correct).

definitions.definition: continuously
examples: I wish you'd stop criticizing me all the time.

definitions.definition: very quickly or very soon
examples: The kids ate their dinner in no time.
We'll be home in next to no time.

definitions.definition: If you say there is or that you have no time to lose, it means that you must do quickly whatever it is that you want to do
examples: Come on, there's no time to lose, we have to get home before John finds out.

definitions.definition: always
examples: I will love you for all time.

definitions.definition: that has ever lived or existed
examples: She's been called the greatest singer of all time.

definitions.definition: the system of recording hours used in different parts of the world
examples: Greenwich Mean Time
daylight saving time

definitions.definition: an amount of time that you have available to do something
examples: I don't know how you find time to do all the things you do.
I thought we'd give her a little more time (= allow her more time) to get the job done.
I'd like to visit all the museums but time is short (= there is little time left).

definitions.definition: If you have time, you have enough time to do something
examples: We don't have much time before the train leaves.
Do you have time for a quick drink after work?
I'd like to learn to sail, but I don't have the time (= I am too busy).
I don't have time to go to the shops today.

definitions.definition: to not make good use of the hours, etc. that you have available
examples: If you'd done your work instead of wasting time on your phone, you'd be finished by now.

definitions.definition: to not have enough hours, etc. available to finish something you are trying to do
examples: She ran out of time and didn't finish the last question.

definitions.definition: to not have enough minutes, etc. available
examples: I'd like to continue this discussion but we're all out of time.

definitions.definition: there are no more minutes, hours, etc. available
examples: OK, everyone, time's up for this week.

definitions.definition: a period of time added to the end of a football match because play was stopped during the game, usually to take care of players who were hurt
examples: His goal in the third minute of time added on sealed the match.

definitions.definition: a particular period of time for which something has been happening, or that is needed for something
examples: After a time, it became clear that nobody was interested in coming to the meetings.
They stayed with us for a short time.
That was the best restaurant I've been to for/in a long time (= a long period has gone past since I went to such a good restaurant).
It was some time ago that I last heard from her.
We're going on holiday in two weeks' time (= after two weeks have passed).
During her time (= while she was) in office, the prime minister introduced a large number of changes.
What do you like doing in your spare/free time (= when you are not working)?

definitions.definition: to stop work, in order to do something else
examples: I asked my boss if I could have some time off (from work) to go to the dentist.

definitions.definition: for a short period
examples: For a time, we all thought that Sheila and Frank would get married.

definitions.definition: for a fairly long period of time
examples: I've been doing yoga for some time.

definitions.definition: for a limited period
examples: Leave the ironing for the time being - I'll do it later.

definitions.definition: used to refer to someone's period (= the bleeding from the womb that happens once a month when a person is not pregnant), especially when you want to avoid saying the word "period"
examples: She often gets stomach pain when it's her time of the month.
Her mother told her she needed to eat red meat because you lose more iron at that time of the month.

definitions.definition: a particular point in the day, as expressed in hours and minutes or shown on a clock, or a particular point in time
examples: What time is it?
"What's the time?" "It's ten o'clock."
What time do you finishwork?
Have you got the time? (= Do you know what time it is?)
He's teaching his daughter to tell the time (= to recognize what time it is by looking at a clock).
Did you find out the times of the trains to Kiev?
The estimated time of arrival/departure of this flight is 11.15.
Oh dear, is that the (right) time? (= is it really so late?)
We always have dinner at the same time every day.
I was exhausted by the time (= when) I got home.
When would be a good time for me to call you?
"What would be the best time of day for us to deliver the table?" "Oh, any time will be OK."
Today's temperatures will be normal for the time of year (= will be as they are expected to be in this season).
Just think, this time (= at the same particular point during) next week we'll be in Aruba.
We regret that at the present time (                    US also at this time) we are unable to supply the products you ordered.
The time is fast drawing near/approaching (= it will soon be the time) when we'll have to make a decision.

definitions.definition: at the particular point when something was thought of or done
examples: It seemed like a good idea at the time.

definitions.definition: If two things happen at the same time, they happen together
examples: We arrived at the same time.

definitions.definition: at a person's present age
examples: At his time of life, he ought to be taking things easy.

definitions.definition: a particular point of the day, year, etc. that is suitable for a particular activity, or at which something is expected to happen
examples: holiday time
party time
Put your toys away now - it's time for bed.
It's time (that) I was leaving.
Is it time (for us) to go home yet?
This is not the time (= not a suitable moment) to be thinking about buying a house.
This is no time (= not a suitable moment) to change your mind.
I feel that the time has come (= now is a suitable moment) for me to move on.
The repairs to the road were finished two weeks ahead of time (= sooner than was expected).
Why is it that the buses never run on time (= make their journeys in the expected number of hours, etc.)?
She's grown old before her time (= sooner than she might have been expected to have done).

definitions.definition: early enough
examples: I got home just in time - it's starting to rain.
If we don't hurry up, we won't be in time to catch the train.
We got there in plenty of time (= we arrived early) for the beginning of the game.

definitions.definition: happening or done at the particular moment that it was expected to happen or be done
examples: My parents go to the house right on time.

definitions.definition: earlier than a particular moment
examples: Let's meet for lunch. I'll call you ahead of time to decide exactly when and where.

definitions.definition: If it is about time/high time that someone did something, it should have been done sooner or a long time ago
examples: It's about time (that) the school improved its food service.
It is high time for the critics to open their minds to a new approach.

definitions.definition: said when someone does something or something happens that you think should have been done or have happened much sooner
examples: "So Ben's finally found a job." "Yeah, it's about time."

definitions.definition: it is the most suitable moment to do something or for something to happen
examples: I haven't told him yet, but I will when the time is right.
She felt the time was right to leave.
The time is ripe for investing in new technology.

definitions.definition: an occasion when something happens, or the experience connected with it
examples: The last time we went to Paris, it rained every day.
Every time/Each time I ask you to do something you say you're busy.
They go swimming three or four times a week.
There are times when I wish I didn't live where I do.
The four-times champion (= the champion on four occasions in the past) was defeated in the second round.
If I'd known at the time (= then) that she was his former wife, I'd never have said what I did.
Sometimes I enjoy my English classes, but at other times I find them really boring.
For the umpteenth/hundredth/thousandth time, (= I've told you on many occasions to) stop teasing your sister.
Did you have a bad/good time (= an unpleasant/enjoyable experience) at the conference?
She had an easy/hard time of it (= a comfortable/uncomfortable experience) with the birth of her second baby.

definitions.definition: sometimes, but not often
examples: From time to time I still think of her.

definitions.definition: again and again
examples: Time after time she gets involved in relationships with the wrong men.

definitions.definition: very often
examples: I've told you time and time again - look before you cross the road.

definitions.definition: continuously
examples: When you're at the airport, you should make sure you have your luggage with you at all times.

definitions.definition: at or during any particular point or moment in the day
examples: Only a certain number of people are allowed in the building at any one time.

definitions.definition: sometimes
examples: You can be really annoying at times, you know.

definitions.definition: ever
examples: Parking is not allowed here at any time.

definitions.definition: on many occasions
examples: The times I've told you, ask before you borrow my clothes.

definitions.definition: a period in history
examples: "A Tale of Two Cities" is set at the time of the French Revolution.
In/During medieval times, women thought to be witches were burned at the stake.
In times gone by, all crops were harvested by hand.
Times were hard (= living conditions were not good) when I was a boy.
He is widely regarded as one of the best writers of modern/our times (= the present or very recent past).
I never thought it would happen in my time (= before I died).
We sat and talked about old times (= things that had happened to us in the past.)

definitions.definition: in the past
examples: At one time, George Eliot lived here.

definitions.definition: having new ideas, opinions, or ways of living long before most other people do
If something is before your time, it happened or existed before you were born or were old enough to remember it
examples: I don't remember the Beatles - they were before my time.

definitions.definition: said to mean that there was a period in the past when something used to happen or be true
examples: Time was (when) you could buy a loaf of bread for sixpence.

definitions.definition: Your time in a race is the number of minutes, hours, etc. you take to complete it
examples: Her time for the marathon was just under three hours.
He ran the 100 metres in record time.

definitions.definition: the number of beats in a bar of music, or the speed at which a piece of music is intended to be played
examples: This piece is written in 4/4 time.

It seemed to me as if the violins were playing out of time (= at a different speed from the other instruments playing the same piece of music).
To beat time is to make a regular series of sounds at the same speed as a piece of music is played.
Tapping your foot will help you to keep time (= to play the music at the correct speed).

definitions.definition: to spend a period of time in prison
examples: It's not always easy to find a job after you've done time.

definitions.definition: to decide that something will happen at a particular time

definitions.definition: to arrange something so that it happens at the best possible time

definitions.definition: to measure how long it takes for something to happen or for someone to do something
examples: Will you time me to see how long it takes me to swim a length?

definitions.definition: to walk on your toes with the heel of your foot lifted off the ground, especially in order not to make a noise

definitions.definition: excellent; perfect

definitions.definition: a long, angry speech expressing strong disapproval

used before a verb to show that it is in the infinitive
used after some verbs, especially when the action described in the infinitive will happen later
examples: She agreed to help.
I'll have to tell him.
Sadly, she didn't live to see her grandchildren.

definitions.definition: used after many verbs of agreeing, needing, and wanting
examples: I need to eat something first.
I'd love to live in New York.
That child ought to be in bed.

definitions.definition: used instead of repeating a verb clause
examples: "Are you going tonight?" "I'm certainly hoping to."

definitions.definition: used in phrases where there are reported orders and requests
examples: He told me to wait.
Did anyone ask Daniel to reserve the room?

definitions.definition: used after some adjectives
examples: It's not likely to happen.
Three months is too long to wait.
She's not strong enough to go hiking up mountains.

definitions.definition: used after some nouns
examples: He has this enviable ability to ignore everything that's unpleasant in life.
This will be my second attempt to make flaky pastry.

definitions.definition: A clause containing to + infinitive can be used as the subject of a sentence
examples: To go overseas on your own is very brave.
My plan was to get it all arranged before I told anyone.

definitions.definition: used after question words
examples: I don't know what to do.
Can you tell me how to get there?

definitions.definition: used with an infinitive to express use or purpose
examples: I'm going there to see my sister.
This tool is used to make holes in leather.
To make this cake, you'll need two eggs, 175 grams of sugar, and 175 grams of flour.
He works to get paid, not because he enjoys it.

definitions.definition: You can introduce a clause with a phrase containing to + infinitive
examples: To be honest (= speaking honestly), Becky, I like thegrey shirt better.
To tell you the truth, I never really liked the man.

definitions.definition: used with an infinitive after 'there is' or 'there are' and a noun
examples: There's an awful lot of work to be done.

definitions.definition: To be going on with means in order to continue with the present activity or situation
examples: Do we have enough paint to be going on with, or should I get some more while I'm out?

definitions.definition: in the direction of
examples: We're going to town on the bus, okay?
We went to Prague last year.
I asked someone the way to the town centre.
You can walk from here to the school in under ten minutes.
I asked Kirsten and Kai to dinner (= invited them to come and eat dinner with me) next week.
We received another invitation to a wedding this morning.
I had my back to them, so I couldn't see what they were doing.
She walked over to the window.
He went up to a complete stranger and started talking.
You've got your sweater on back to front (= with the back of the sweater on the chest).

definitions.definition: used for showing who receives something or who experiences an action
examples: I lent my bike to my brother.
I told that to Alex and he just laughed.
Who's the letter addressed to?

definitions.definition: With many verbs that have two objects, 'to' can be used before the indirect object
examples: Give me that gun./Give that gun to me.

definitions.definition: until a particular time, state, or level is reached
examples: It's only two weeks to Christmas.
Unemployment has risen to almost eight million.
He drank himself to death.
She nursed me back to health.

definitions.definition: used when saying the time, to mean before the stated hour
examples: It's twenty to six.

definitions.definition: used to suggest an extreme state
examples: Look at your shirt - it's torn to shreds!
She was thrilled to bits.
I was bored to tears.

definitions.definition: in connection with
examples: What was their response to your query?
She was so mean to me.
There's a funny side to everything.

definitions.definition: used to say where something is fastened or connected
examples: The paper was stuck to the wall with tape.
A fast rail service connects us to the city.

definitions.definition: used before an infinitive, usually with 'be', to indicate a future action
examples: The government announced today that it is to cut funding for the arts for next year.

definitions.definition: used in this pattern to say what someone should do or to give an order
examples: You're not to (= you must not) bite your nails like that.

definitions.definition: Newspapers often use to + infinitive without 'be' in their headlines (= titles of articles) when reporting planned future events
examples: Russia to send troops in.

definitions.definition: causing a particular feeling in a particular person
examples: That's when I found out, to my amazement, that she was coming here.

definitions.definition: considered by
examples: I realize it may sound strange to you.
I mean, £50 is nothing to him (= he would not consider it a large amount).
"I hear you've been going out with Ella." "Yeah, so? What's it to you?" (= It should not interest you, and you have no right to ask about it.)

definitions.definition: serving
examples: As a personal trainer to the rich and famous, he earns over a million dollars a year.

definitions.definition: against or very near
examples: Stand back to back.
They were dancing cheek to cheek.

definitions.definition: matching or belonging to
examples: My dad gave me the keys to his car.
I've lost the trousers to this jacket.

definitions.definition: having as a characteristic feature
examples: She has a mean side to her.
There is a very moral tone to this book.

definitions.definition: used to show the position of something or someone in comparison with something or someone else
examples: John's standing to the left of Adrian in the photo.

definitions.definition: in honour or memory of
examples: I proposed a toast to the bride and the groom.
The record is dedicated to her mother, who died recently.

definitions.definition: for each
examples: How many dollars are there to the pound?

definitions.definition: used in phrases that show a range
examples: There were probably 30 to 35 (= a number between 30 and 35) people there.

definitions.definition: at the same time as music or other sound
examples: I like exercising to music.
He left the stage to the sound of booing.

definitions.definition: relating to a positive reaction or result
examples: When the pasta is done to your liking, drain the water.
I think being at the meeting would be to your advantage.

definitions.definition: compared with
examples: She's earning a reasonable wage, but nothing to what she could if she was in the private sector.
Paul beat me by three games to two (= he won three and I won two).
He was old enough to be her father - she looked about 30 to his 60.

definitions.definition: into a closed position
examples: I'll just push the door to.

definitions.definition: the last in a series of good or bad events, remarks, actions, etc., especially when this is even better or worse than everything that has gone before

examples: The markings and designs have totemic significance.
The car industry has a totemic status that is out of proportion to its real economic significance.

definitions.definition: an object that is respected by a group of people, especially for religious reasons
examples: Television could be seen as a totem of modern society.

definitions.definition: used as a short form of totally to emphasize what you are saying
examples: You're totes right.
I am totes going to buy those shoes.

definitions.definition: present participle of tote
to carry something, especially something heavy or awkward
examples: She usually toted the baby around in a backpack.
The building was surrounded with bodyguards toting sub-machine guns.
Gun-toting security men were posted at all the entrances.

definitions.definition: a young child
examples: These are good, strong toys for tiny tots.

definitions.definition: a small drink of alcohol
examples: He poured them each a generous tot of whisky/rum.

definitions.definition: to walk with difficulty in a way that looks as if you are about to fall
examples: She tottered unsteadily down the stairs in her high-heeled shoes.

definitions.definition: to shake and move from side to side
examples: Several tall piles of books tottered and fell.

definitions.definition: (of a company, government, etc.) to become weaker and less likely to carry on existing
examples: The industry has tottered from crisis to crisis now for two years.

definitions.definition: a South American bird that has a brightly coloured beak

to put your hand or another part of your body lightly onto and off something or someone
examples: That paint is wet - don't touch (it).
He touched the girl on the arm to get her attention.
The boy touched the worm with (= using, in his hand) a twig.
The setting sun touched the trees with red (= made them appear red for a short time).

definitions.definition: (of two or more things) to be so close together that there is no space between; to be in contact
examples: He fell asleep as soon as his head touched the pillow.
She pushed the two bookcases together until they touched/were touching.

definitions.definition: to harm someone, or use or damage something
examples: Please don't touch any of my stuff while I'm away.

definitions.definition: (usually used in negative sentences) to eat or drink something
examples: They didn't touch any of the food we left for them.
Honestly, I haven't touched a drop (= drunk any alcohol) all night.

definitions.definition: to influence someone or something emotionally, or cause feelings of sympathy in someone
examples: Tragedy touched their lives when their son was 16.
The TV report about the children's work for charity touched thousands of people's hearts.

definitions.definition: (usually used in negative sentences) to have or reach the same standard (= level of quality) as someone or something
examples: Her novels can't touch (= are not as good as) those of her sister.
There's no one to touch him as an illustrator of children's books.

definitions.definition: a small amount
examples: "Would you like milk?" "Just a touch."
There was a touch of irony/humour in her voice.

definitions.definition: To show that an illness is not too serious, you can say you have had a touch of it
examples: I had a touch of flu/hay fever.

definitions.definition: a small addition or detail that makes something better
examples: The speech had several comic touches.
Using a sailing ship as the company logo was a touch of genius (= a good/clever idea or action).
The flowers on the table provided the finishing touch.

definitions.definition: slightly
examples: The weather has turned a touch too cold for my liking.

definitions.definition: the ability to know what something is like by feeling it with the fingers
examples: the sense of touch
I found the right coin in the dark by touch.

definitions.definition: used after an adjective to express how something feels when you put your hand on it
examples: The material was soft to the touch.

definitions.definition: a quick, light movement of one thing, especially a hand, onto and off another thing
examples: I felt a cold touch on my arm.
At a/the touch of a button, the door opened.

definitions.definition: to communicate or continue to communicate with someone by using a phone or writing to them
examples: Are you still in touch with any of your old school friends?
Jen and I never kept in touch after college.
We're in close touch with our office in Spain.

definitions.definition: to stop communicating with someone, usually because they do not live near you now
examples: We lost touch over the years.

definitions.definition: an ability to do things in the stated, especially positive, way
examples: He has a deft touch with tricky painting jobs.
She gave the job her own special/magic/professional/personal touch.
I admire her lightness/sureness of touch as a cook.
He used to be a good writer but I think he's losing his touch.

definitions.definition: in sports such as football, a player's ability to control the ball and make it do what they want, or an occasion when a player controls the ball with their foot
examples: Nicolas Anelka squandered the chance to score when his touch let him down in front of goal.
Hazard took several touches before crossing.

definitions.definition: the area outside either of the long edges of the space on which football and rugby are played
examples: Playing for safety, he kicked the ball into touch.

definitions.definition: If you are in touch/out of touch with a subject, activity, or situation, your knowledge about it is recent/not recent
examples: He's not really in touch with what young people are interested in.
I didn't see any news all the time I was on holiday, so I'm completely out of touch.

definitions.definition: the landing of an aircraft or of some types of spacecraft
examples: One of the plane's tyres burst on touchdown.

definitions.definition: (in American football) the act of scoring points by carrying the ball across the other team's goal line or throwing the ball so that it is caught by a member of your team who is across the other team's goal line
(in rugby) the act of scoring points by putting the ball down on the ground behind the other team's goal

definitions.definition: behaving in an unusual and strange way

definitions.definition: in a way that shows someone is easily offended or upset
examples: Very firmly and touchily, he declined to comment.

definitions.definition: the fact of needing to be dealt with carefully because people may be offended or upset
examples: Everyone is aware of the touchiness of the situation .

definitions.definition: the quality of being easily offended or upset
examples: My opponent's touchiness every time he is questioned should make us more concerned.

definitions.definition: making you feel sadness, sympathy, etc.

definitions.definition: in a way that makes you feel emotion, especially sadness or sympathy
examples: He spoke touchingly about how important he felt this work was.

definitions.definition: controlled by movement or sound, rather than by a keyboard, button or other device that you need to touch
examples: They introduced touchless water taps and flushes.
These cameras are gesture-controlled or "touchless".

definitions.definition: using a process where one object does not touch another, or where something is touched only by water
examples: The touchless car wash uses water jets and detergent, but no brushes, minimizing damage to the vehicle.

definitions.definition: one of the two lines that show the position of the long edges of the area in which particular games, such as football, are played
examples: She spends her Saturday afternoons standing on the touchline, watching her boyfriend play rugby.

definitions.definition: an established standard or principle by which something is judged

definitions.definition: strong; not easily broken or made weaker
examples: These toys are made from tough plastic.
Children's shoes need to be tough.

definitions.definition: of a person, able to deal with difficult situations and not be easily defeated, frightened or upset
examples: You have to be tough to be successful in politics.
Even tough guys need to cry sometimes.
Their lawyer is a real tough customer (= person).

definitions.definition: severe in limiting what is allowed or in punishing people who do not obey rules or laws
examples: Tough new safety standards have been introduced for cars.
There have been calls for tougher controls/restrictions on what online retailers are allowed to sell.
I think it's time the police got tougher on/with (= treated more severely) people who drink and drive.
The government is continuing to take a tough line on terrorism.

definitions.definition: difficult to do or to deal with
examples: They've had an exceptionally tough life.
They will be a tough team to beat.
The company is going through a tough time at the moment.
We've had to make some very tough decisions.
My boss has given me a tough job/assignment.
Many homeless people are facing a tough winter.
After some tough bargaining, we finally agreed on a deal.

definitions.definition: Tough food is difficult to cut or eat
examples: This steak is very tough.
These apples have tough skins.

definitions.definition: likely to be violent or to contain violence; not kind or pleasant
examples: a tough neighbourhood
Many of the country's toughest criminals are held in this prison.

definitions.definition: unlucky
examples: "I have to work late today because I'm very behind on my work." "Oh, tough luck!"
It's tough on Geoff that he's going to miss the party.

definitions.definition: sometimes used to show that you have no sympathy for someone's problems or difficulties
examples: "I don't have any money left." "Well, (that's just) tough - you shouldn't have spent it all on cigarettes."

definitions.definition: a violent person
examples: Bands of armed toughs roamed the city.

definitions.definition: to make something or someone tough, strong, or stronger
examples: The UN announced its intentions to toughen sanctions still further.
The government wants to toughen (up) the existing drug laws/controls.
His time in the army certainly toughened him up.
Car windows are usually made from toughened glass.

definitions.definition: to become difficult to deal with
examples: Amid toughening competition in the market, many firms are going out of business.

a visit to a place or area, especially one during which you look around the place or area and learn about it
examples: We went on a guided tour of the cathedral/museum/factory.
A bus took us on a sightseeing tour of the city.
a tour guide

definitions.definition: a journey made for pleasure, especially as a holiday, visiting several different places in an area
examples: a cycling tour of Provence
They've just come back from a tour of (                    UK also round) California's wine country.
Tour operators (= companies which arrange holidays for people) have reported a drop in sales.

definitions.definition: a planned visit to several places in a country or area made for a special purpose, such as one made by a politician, sports team, or group of performers
examples: a lecture/concert tour
The Queen is making a two-week tour of Australia.
She is performing in Birmingham tonight, on the third leg of (= stage of) her nationwide tour.
The England cricket team is on tour in Pakistan.

definitions.definition: a tour of duty
to go on a tour somewhere
examples: We spent a month touring (around/round/in) Kenya.
The New Zealand team will be touring (in) Europe this winter.
The president toured US military bases yesterday.
The band is currently touring to promote the new album.

definitions.definition: If a play tours a particular area, it is performed in several places there
examples: The play will be performed first in the capital, and will then tour the rest of the country.

definitions.definition: If a play tours a particular area, it is performed in several places there

definitions.definition: (of musicians, sports teams, exhibitions, etc.) going to several places in a country or area to perform, compete, be seen, etc.
examples: a touring opera company
It was the world's top touring band, grossing more than $26 million that year.
She's the subject of a touring exhibition that will come to Washington in April.
He told the audience he was tired from a busy touring schedule.

definitions.definition: relating to a journey to visit several places in a country or area for pleasure, especially as a holiday
examples: Explore the real America on a touring holiday through Washington State and Oregon.

definitions.definition: the activity of visiting several places in a country or area to perform, compete, be seen, etc.
examples: The band has built an extensive fan base through relentless touring.
After almost 50 years of recording and touring, why do you still do it?

definitions.definition: the activity of visiting several places in a country or area for pleasure, especially as a holiday
examples: Touring is a marvellous, carefree way to get to know Italy.
There is no better place than Colorado for motorcycle touring.

definitions.definition: the business of providing services such as transport, places to stay, or entertainment for people who are on holiday
examples: Tourism is Venice's main industry.
These beautiful old towns have remained relatively untouched by tourism.

definitions.definition: someone who visits a place for pleasure and interest, usually while on holiday
examples: Millions of tourists visit Rome every year.
Hordes (= very large groups) of tourists flock to the Mediterranean each year.
Florida has a number of major tourist attractions.
The island is very busy during the tourist season.

definitions.definition: a member of a sports team who is travelling from place to place in a foreign country, playing games
examples: The West Indies easily defeated the tourists.

definitions.definition: for or relating to tourists (= people who visit a place for pleasure and interest, usually while on holiday)
examples: touristic excursions

The island is very busy during the tourist season.

definitions.definition: a member of a sports team who is travelling from place to place in a foreign country, playing games

definitions.definition: A touristy place is not attractive because a lot of tourists visit it and it is full of things for them to buy and do
examples: This used to be a pretty little fishing town, but now it's become very touristy.

definitions.definition: a competition for teams or single players in which a series of games is played, and the winners of each game play against each other until only one winner is left
examples: a tennis/chess/golf tournament
They were defeated in the first round of the tournament.

examples: a tennis/chess/golf tournament

definitions.definition: a strip of cloth that is tied tightly around an injured arm or leg to stop it bleeding
examples: If it continues to bleed, you may have to apply a tourniquet to the limb.

definitions.definition: a strip of cloth that is tied tightly around an injured arm or leg to stop it bleeding

definitions.definition: a visit to a place or area, especially one during which you look around the place or area and learn about it
examples: We went on a guided tour of the cathedral/museum/factory.
A bus took us on a sightseeing tour of the city.
a tour guide

definitions.definition: a journey made for pleasure, especially as a holiday, visiting several different places in an area
examples: a cycling tour of Provence
They've just come back from a tour of (                    UK also round) California's wine country.
Tour operators (= companies which arrange holidays for people) have reported a drop in sales.

definitions.definition: a planned visit to several places in a country or area made for a special purpose, such as one made by a politician, sports team, or group of performers
examples: a lecture/concert tour
The Queen is making a two-week tour of Australia.
She is performing in Birmingham tonight, on the third leg of (= stage of) her nationwide tour.
The England cricket team is on tour in Pakistan.

definitions.definition: a tour of duty
to go on a tour somewhere
examples: We spent a month touring (around/round/in) Kenya.
The New Zealand team will be touring (in) Europe this winter.
The president toured US military bases yesterday.
The band is currently touring to promote the new album.

definitions.definition: If a play tours a particular area, it is performed in several places there
examples: The play will be performed first in the capital, and will then tour the rest of the country.

definitions.definition: to make someone's hair untidy, for example by rubbing it, or, of the wind, by blowing it
examples: She tousled her niece's hair, and went out.
The wind tousled their hair.

definitions.definition: having hair that looks untidy, as if it has been rubbed
examples: Naomi stood in front of them, her face flushed, her hair tousled.
He came to breakfast, all tousled, in last night's clothes.

definitions.definition: to advertise, talk about, or praise something or someone repeatedly, especially as a way of encouraging people to like, accept, or buy something
examples: The minister has been touting these ideas for some time.
He is being widely touted as the next leader of the party.
A local car dealership was touting its services/wares on the radio.

definitions.definition: to repeatedly try to persuade people to buy your goods or services
examples: There were hundreds of taxis at the airport, all touting for business/custom.

definitions.definition: to sell tickets for something such as a sports game or theatre performance unofficially, usually at a much higher price than the official price
examples: £30 seats for the match were being touted for £500.

definitions.definition: a person who touts tickets
examples: Britain's best-known ticket tout once boasted that he could get you tickets for anything.

definitions.definition: to pull a car, boat, etc. along, fastened behind another vehicle or boat
examples: You shouldn't drive fast when your car is towing a caravan.
The road was closed while the vehicles that had been involved in the accident were towed away/off.
The damaged boat was towed to safety.

definitions.definition: to take away a vehicle that has been parked illegally using an official truck, to a place from which the driver has to pay to pick it up
examples: You're not allowed to park here - your car will be towed away .
This part of town is a tow zone (= one in which cars left illegally will be removed).

definitions.definition: to pull someone's vehicle using a rope or chain tied to your vehicle
examples: When my car broke down, a police car gave me a tow to the nearest garage.

definitions.definition: being pulled along
examples: The car in front of us is on tow - that's why we're going so slowly.

definitions.definition: in the direction of, or closer to someone or something
examples: She stood up and walked toward him.
He leaned toward his wife and whispered, "Can we go home soon?"
She kept glancing toward the phone.
The country seems to be drifting toward war.
There is a trend toward healthier eating among all sectors of the population.

definitions.definition: in relation to something or someone
examples: They've always been very friendly toward me.
There has been a change in government policy toward energy efficiency.
He feels a lot of anger/hostility/antagonism/animosity toward his father.
Most newspapers are biased toward one particular political party.

definitions.definition: near to, just before, or around a time or place
examples: Our seats were toward the back of the theater.
I often get hungry toward the middle of the morning.
We're getting toward winter, and it's getting dark earlier.

definitions.definition: for the purpose of buying or achieving something
examples: I'm saving up to buy a car, and Dad has given me some money toward it.
Would you like to make a contribution (= give some money) toward a present for Linda?

to find someone or something that was lost
examples: The police are trying to trace the mother of a newborn baby found abandoned outside a hospital.
Attempts to trace the whereabouts of a man seen leaving the scene of the crime have so far been unsuccessful.
Their missing daughter was finally traced to (= found in) Manchester.

definitions.definition: to find the origin of something
examples: The phone company was unable to trace the call.
No one has yet been able to trace the source of the rumour.

definitions.definition: to discover the causes or origins of something by examining the way in which it has developed
examples: The outbreak of food poisoning was traced to some contaminated shellfish.
She has traced her family history back to the seventeenth century.
The practice of giving eggs at Easter can be traced back to (= first happened in) festivals in ancient China.

definitions.definition: to describe the way in which something has developed
examples: The film traces the events leading up to the Russian Revolution in 1917.

definitions.definition: to copy a drawing or pattern, etc. by drawing over its lines through a thin piece of transparent paper
examples: Did you draw this picture yourself, or did you trace it?
She learned to write her name by tracing out the letters.

definitions.definition: to draw a shape by showing the main or outer lines
examples: The child was tracing patterns in/on the sand with a stick.

definitions.definition: a sign that something has happened or existed
examples: He attempted to cover up all the traces of his crime.
When she moved out, she left no trace of having been there.
My wallet has been missing for several days and I can't find any trace of it.
He seems to have vanished without (a) trace (= no one knows where he is).

definitions.definition: an act of finding information about something electronically, or the record of the information found in this way
examples: The phone company put a trace on the call.

definitions.definition: a very slight amount
examples: Traces of drugs were found in his blood.
There is just a trace of grey in his hair.
She speaks English without any trace of an accent.
There was the faintest trace of a smile on her lips.
"How wonderful!" she said, without any trace of sarcasm.

definitions.definition: the ability to discover information about where and how a product was made
examples: Labelling and traceability of food are key issues.

definitions.definition: the ability to find or follow something
examples: The traceability of electronic cash could help rid society of the scourge of crime.

definitions.definition: possible to trace
examples: In theory, most phone calls should be traceable.
His medical problems were shown to be traceable to (= to have been caused by) his having been exposed to dangerous chemicals.

definitions.definition: a sport in which people compete with each other by running a race on a specially prepared circular path

a mark or line of marks left on the ground or on another surface by an animal, person, or vehicle that has moved over it, showing the direction they moved in

definitions.definition: to be examining marks or pieces of information that show where a person or animal has gone, in order to catch him, her, or it
examples: The police are on the track of the killer.

definitions.definition: the direction that something has taken or will take through the air
examples: People living in the track of the hurricane have been advised to leave their homes until it has passed.

definitions.definition: the direction in which someone's job or education develops
examples: She was a lawyer, but then she changed track completely and became a doctor.

definitions.definition: the way in which a thought or idea has developed or might develop

definitions.definition: to start talking about something that is not part of what you should be talking about

definitions.definition: a group of school students with similar ability who are approximately the same age and are taught together

definitions.definition: one of several songs or pieces of music on a CD or other musical recording

definitions.definition: a part of a magnetic strip onto which sound can be recorded, with several tracks on one magnetic strip
examples: When a piece of music is recorded, each instrument is recorded separately on a 24 or 48-track tape.

definitions.definition: to follow a person or animal by looking for proof that they have been somewhere, or by using electronic equipment
examples: It's difficult to track an animal over stony ground.

definitions.definition: to record the progress or development of something over a period
examples: The study tracked the careers of 1,226 doctors who trained at the Medical School.

definitions.definition: If a film or video camera tracks in a particular direction, it moves along while it is filming
examples: The movie ends with a long tracking shot around the deserted house.

definitions.definition: If a moving part of a recording machine tracks, it gets into the correct position for operating
examples: Our VCR tracks automatically.

definitions.definition: to follow the level of an interest rate, share price, etc.
examples: The mortgage deal will track the Bank of England base rate plus 0.75 percent.

definitions.definition: to group and teach together school students with similar abilities who are approximately the same age

definitions.definition: a very sad event or situation, especially one involving death or suffering
examples: The pilot averted a tragedy when he succeeded in preventing the plane from crashing.

It's a tragedy (that) so many young people are unable to find jobs.

definitions.definition: a play about death or suffering with a sad end, or this type of play generally
examples: Shakespeare's tragedies include "Hamlet", "King Lear", and "Othello".

problems or difficulties
examples: The tax forms were complicated and I had a lot of trouble with them.
Their problems seem to be over for the moment, but there could be more trouble ahead.
The trouble started/began when my father came to live with us.
Parents often have trouble finding good carers for their children.
We started holding meetings by phone, as travelling in and out of the city became too much trouble.
I should get it finished over the weekend without too much trouble.
You'll only be storing up trouble for the future if you don't go to the dentist now.
She thought her troubles would be over once she'd got divorced.
His birthday is the least of my troubles at the moment - I don't even have enough money to pay the rent.
Most of the current troubles stem from (= are caused by) our new computer system.

definitions.definition: a situation in which you experience problems, usually because of something you have done wrong or badly
examples: He's never been in trouble with his teachers before.
She'll be in big trouble if she scratches Sam's car.
He got into financial trouble after his divorce.
I hope you won't get into trouble because of what I said to your dad.
The camp is a great way of getting kids off the street and keeping them out of trouble.
I hope I haven't landed you in trouble with your boss.
The marriage ran into trouble because of her husband's heavy drinking.
The company will be in serious/real trouble if we lose this contract.
He's stayed out of trouble since he was released from jail last year.

definitions.definition: a characteristic of someone or something that is considered a disadvantage or problem
examples: The trouble with this carpet is that it gets dirty very easily.
It's a brilliant idea. The only trouble is that we don't know how much it will cost.
Ron's trouble is that he's too impatient.

definitions.definition: problems or difficulties caused by something failing to operate as it should
examples: The plane developed engine trouble shortly after take-off.
They have a good reputation for building reliable trouble-free cars.
Her knee trouble is expected to keep her out of the game for the rest of the season.

definitions.definition: problems in the form of arguments, fighting, or violence
examples: Listen, I don't want any trouble in here, so please just finish your drink and leave.
My parents said we had to leave at the first sign of trouble.
My little brother's always trying to stir up (= create) trouble between me and my boyfriend.

definitions.definition: slight problems or effort
examples: I didn't mean to cause you any trouble.
"I'd love some more coffee, if it isn't too much trouble." "Oh, it's no trouble at all."
I don't want to put you to any trouble (= create any work for you).
If you took the trouble to listen to what I was saying, you'd know what I was talking about.
They went to a lot of trouble (= made a lot of effort) for their dinner party, but half the guests didn't bother to turn up.
It's annoying, but I don't think I'll go to the trouble of making an official complaint.

definitions.definition: to cause someone to be worried or nervous
examples: I asked her what was troubling her, but she didn't want to talk.
Many of us are deeply troubled by the chairman's decision.
It troubles me (that) you didn't discuss your problems with me earlier.

definitions.definition: to cause someone to have a problem or difficulties
examples: He has been troubled by a knee injury for most of the season.

definitions.definition: to cause someone a small amount of effort
examples: May I trouble you for (= please give me) some more wine, please?
Could I trouble you to open that window? I can't reach it.

definitions.definition: (especially of facts or statements) right and not wrong; correct
examples: Is it true that Mariana and Mark are getting married?
The allegations, if true, could lead to her resignation.
Her story is only partly true.
Would it be true to say that you've never liked Jim?
I suspect she gave a true picture (= accurate description) of what had happened.
I don't believe these exam results are a true reflection of your abilities.
The movie is based on the true story of a London gangster.
She has since admitted that her earlier statement was not strictly (= completely) true.
It used to be very cheap but that's no longer true (= that situation does not now exist).
Alcohol should be consumed in moderation, and this is particularly true for pregnant women.
Parents of young children often become depressed, and this is especially true of single parents.

definitions.definition: correct or accurate but not completely explaining something
examples: It's true enough that he had doubts about the project, but we have to look further to understand why he resigned.

definitions.definition: being what exists, rather than what was thought, intended, or stated
examples: true love
a true friend
There cannot be true democracy without reform of the electoral system.
The true horror of the accident did not become clear until the morning.

definitions.definition: If a hope, wish, or dream comes true, it happens although it was unlikely that it would
examples: I'd always dreamed of owning my own house, but I never thought it would come true.
After all the problems I'd had getting pregnant, Oliver's birth was a dream come true.

definitions.definition: sincere or loyal, and likely to continue to be so in difficult situations
examples: There are few true believers in communism left in the party.
She has vowed to remain true to the president whatever happens.
He said he'd repay the money the next day, and true to his word (= as he had promised), he gave it all back to me the following morning.

definitions.definition: to behave according to your beliefs and do what you think is right
Someone who does something true to form or type behaves as other people would have expected from previous experience
examples: True to form, when it came to his turn to buy the drinks, he said he'd left his wallet at home.

definitions.definition: having all the characteristics necessary to be accurately described as something
examples: Only true deer have antlers.
It was said that the portrait was a very true likeness of her (= looked very much like her).
In true Hollywood style (= in a way that is typical of Hollywood), she's had four marriages and three facelifts.

definitions.definition: fitted or positioned accurately
examples: None of the drawers were true.

definitions.definition: to not be in the correct position or to be slightly bent out of the correct shape
examples: This door won't shut. I think the frame must be out of true.

definitions.definition: straight and without moving to either side
examples: Make sure you hit the nails in true.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) move in a circle around a fixed point or line
examples: The earth turns on its axis once every 24 hours.

The wheels started to turn (around).
Turn the steering wheel as quickly as you can.
She turned the doorknob and quietly opened the door.
Slowly, I turned the door handle.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) change the direction in which you are facing or moving
examples: Turn right at the traffic lights.

We have to turn down/into/up the next road on the right.
Plants tend to turn towards the source of light.

He turned round and waved to us.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) become, change into, or come to be something
examples: The weather has suddenly turned cold.

definitions.definition: a main road that you usually have to pay to use
examples: the New Jersey Turnpike

definitions.definition: → turnaround
→ turnaround
to (cause to) move in a circle around a fixed point or line
examples: The earth turns on its axis once every 24 hours.
She turned on her toes, holding out her skirt.
The wheels started to turn (around).
Turn the steering wheel as quickly as you can.
She turned the doorknob and quietly opened the door.
Slowly, I turned the door handle.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) change the direction in which you are facing or moving
examples: Turn right at the traffic lights.
The path twists and turns for the next half mile.
We have to turn down/into/up the next road on the right.
Plants tend to turn towards the source of light.
She turned to face him.
He turned round and waved to us.
He turned on his heel (= turned quickly to face the opposite direction) and left the room.
The person on my left turned to me and whispered "Not another speech!"
His wife tried to speak to him, but he turned his back (on her)/turned away (from her) (= moved so that his back, not his face, was towards her to show his anger).
At about three o'clock, the tide started to turn (= the sea started to come closer to or move away from the beach).
He turned his head to me to listen.
I'll just turn the car round and go back the way we came.
We watched until the car had turned (= gone around) the corner.
The army turned their guns on (= pointed them at and started to shoot at) the protesters.
She can turn (= perform) a somersault.

definitions.definition: to move, or to move an object or page, so that a different side or surface is on the top
examples: Now turn the page, please, and start work on Exercise 2.
She turned the vase over to look for the price.
He turned over two or three pages.
She put out the light, turned over (= rolled in order to face in another direction) and went to sleep.
Now turn to (= open the book at) page 23 and look at the first paragraph.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) become, change into, or come to be something
examples: The weather has suddenly turned cold.
When I refused to pay, he turned nasty.
She turned pale and started to shiver.
The mood of the meeting turned solemn when the extent of the problem became known.
The companies worked well together for a while, but eventually the relationship turned sour (= became bad).
Keele, pop star turned business tycoon, has launched a new range of cosmetics.
The dry weather turned the soil into/to concrete.
By the end of September, the leaves have started to turn (= become brown).
Her attitude turned from politely interested to enthusiastic during the course of our conversation.

definitions.definition: to become a particular age or time
examples: She turned 18 last year.
It's just turned ten o'clock.

definitions.definition: to use a control to switch a piece of equipment or a power or water supply on or off, or to increase or reduce what it is producing
examples: Turn off/out the light.
Who turned the TV on?
I asked him to turn down the heating.
Turn the sound up - I can't hear what they're saying.
The heater turns off (= can be switched off) using this switch.

definitions.definition: to damage the muscles in the foot by suddenly twisting it too strongly
examples: She turned her ankle on the rocks and had to hobble back to camp.

definitions.definition: to shape a piece of wood while it is attached to a machine that spins it
examples: a turned bowl

definitions.definition: an opportunity or a duty to do something at a particular time or in a particular order, before or after other people
examples: Is it my turn yet?

It's your turn to do the dishes!
In this game if you give the wrong answer you have to miss a turn.

definitions.definition: When a number of people take turns, they do the same thing one after the other

definitions.definition: one after the other in an agreed order

definitions.definition: an action that causes something to move in a circle round a fixed point

definitions.definition: a change in the direction in which you are moving or facing

definitions.definition: government by a ruler or small group of people who have unlimited power over the people in their country or state and use it unfairly and cruelly
examples: This, the president promised us, was a war against tyranny.

definitions.definition: a situation in which someone or something controls how you are able to live, in an unfair way
examples: Women, the play seems to suggest, must resist the tyranny of domesticity.

definitions.definition: a ruler who has unlimited power over other people, and uses it unfairly and cruelly

I keep a spare tyre in the back of the car.
